sourceMovie.title: Kung Fu Hustle
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Kung Fu Hustle for the same reason it broke through in 2004: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a comedy rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Stephen Chow keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The action and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Kick-Ass
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Kung Fu Hustle clicked for you, Kick-Ass (2010) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and crime, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Transporter
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Kung Fu Hustle clicked for you, The Transporter (2002) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and crime, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Enchanted
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Kung Fu Hustle clicked for you, Enchanted (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and fantasy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Kung Fu Hustle?
faq.answer: Try Kick-Ass, The Transporter, Enchanted—each shares DNA with Kung Fu Hustle while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Kung Fu Hustle?
faq.answer: Kung Fu Hustle (2004) is best described as Action, Comedy, Crime, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Kung Fu Hustle worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
